text: It is better to be a human being dissatisfied than a pig satisfied; better to be Socrates dissatisfied than a fool satisfied. And if the fool, or the pig, is of a different opinion, it is because they only know their own side of the question. The other party to the comparison knows both sides.
author: John Stuart Mill
out_pulls_note: Out-pulls the gloss: instead of restating 'greatest happiness for the greatest number,' it carries Mill's qualitative-hedonism move (higher pleasures), the part of the doctrine the bare gloss omits.
search_notes: Found in Gutenberg #11224 (Mill, Utilitarianism), ch. II 'What Utilitarianism Is.' Verbatim contiguous span; byte-confirmed against fetched plain text using the validator's normalisation. The 'Socrates dissatisfied' line is sometimes loosely paraphrased online ('better to be Socrates unsatisfied'); I matched the literal source wording 'dissatisfied'. No verdict words used. High confidence.
